facts.text: Chadwicks began in 1813 when John Chadwick, Abner Brownell, and Ira Todd built the Eagle Cotton Factory and Willowvale Bleachery along Sauquoit Creek.
The village’s name honors founder John Chadwick, an English immigrant who had worked in textile mills in Rhode Island before settling in New York.
In 1905 the Delaware, Lackawanna & Western Railroad built a station on Elm Street, linking Chadwicks to regional trade routes.
The Eagle Cotton Factory later became the Standard Silk Mill before closing, its site now part of Chadwicks town park.
Chadwicks preserves much of its historic mill-era housing, blending 19th‑century architecture with modern living today.
The village’s early textile industry thrived on Sauquoit Creek’s reliable water power, fueling multiple mills throughout the 1800s.
